title: Define application/bolyra-proof+cbor content type and envelope format
description: The protocol lacks a standardized wire format for transmitting proofs between parties. Define a CBOR-based proof envelope with registered IANA media type application/bolyra-proof+cbor, containing fields: proof_system (groth16|plonk), circuit_id, public_signals[], proof_bytes, and metadata (version, timestamp). This enables HTTP-based interop without bespoke JSON parsing and aligns with IETF draft Section 6 (Wire Format) which is currently a stub. Deliver: ABNF grammar, CDDL schema, and a conformance check in the test vectors runner.

title: Complete DID method resolution algorithm per W3C DID Core §7.1
description: spec/did-method-bolyra.md defines the DID syntax (did:bolyra:<commitment>) but lacks the required resolution algorithm: how a resolver maps a DID to a DID Document by querying the IdentityRegistry contract's Merkle roots and revocation status. Implement the read() method per W3C DID Resolution spec, define the DID Document structure (verificationMethod using JsonWebKey2020 for the Baby Jubjub key, service endpoints for handshake initiation), and add deactivated metadata when IdentityRevoked fires. Deliver: updated spec file, a reference resolver in TypeScript, and 5 test vectors (active human, active agent, revoked, not-found, stale-root).

title: Machine-readable conformance test vectors (JSON, 50+ cases)
description: No independent implementation can verify compatibility without canonical test vectors. Create a spec/test-vectors/ directory with JSON files covering: valid handshake (human+agent), expired credential rejection, revoked human nullifier, stale Merkle root (both trees), scope subset violation, cumulative bit encoding violation, delegation chain at depths 1/2/3 and rejection at depth 4, nonce replay, and the phantom delegatee attack (now fixed by CIP-1). Each vector includes: circuit inputs, expected public outputs or expected failure reason, and a ground-truth proof for positive cases. Deliver: JSON schema for test vector format, 50+ vectors, and a runner script that validates against snarkjs.

title: SD-JWT-based session token for off-chain verifiers
description: On-chain verification is expensive and unnecessary for many use cases (API gateways, MCP servers). Define a standardized SD-JWT (IETF draft-ietf-oauth-selective-disclosure-jwt) session token that an on-chain verifier mints after a successful handshake: claims include nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, agentMerkleRoot, and exp. Off-chain verifiers check the JWT signature against the registry's signing key without touching the chain. This bridges Bolyra into the existing OAuth/OIDC ecosystem. Deliver: JWT claim set spec, issuance flow in IdentityRegistry (or a companion Relayer contract), and SDK method verifySessionToken().

title: Add human tree root history buffer matching agent tree's 30-root buffer
description: IdentityRegistry maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but humanTree has no equivalent — any new enrollment immediately invalidates all in-flight human proofs. This is an interop hazard: a compliant prover generating a proof during an enrollment window gets a spurious rejection. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 circular buffer to humanTree with an isKnownHumanRoot() check in verifyHandshake(). This is a one-line architectural parity fix but has significant correctness impact. Deliver: contract change, updated test coverage, and a note in the IETF draft Security Considerations about root staleness windows.

title: Type-safe proof envelope codec with serialize/deserialize
description: The SDK currently passes raw proof arrays and public signal tuples between proveHandshake() and verifyHandshake(). Add a BolyraProofEnvelope type with toJSON()/fromJSON()/toBytes()/fromBytes() methods that bundle the proof, public signals, circuit type, and protocol version into a single portable object. This eliminates the most common integration mistake: misaligning signal indices when passing proofs across service boundaries (HTTP, message queues, MCP tool results).

title: Structured error codes with actionable recovery hints
description: Replace the current generic Error throws in the SDK and contract reverts with a BolyraError class hierarchy: StaleRootError (includes which root and how old), ExpiredCredentialError (includes expiry vs current timestamp), ScopeNarrowingViolation (includes parent vs child bitmask), NullifierReusedError. Each error carries a `code` string (e.g. 'BOLYRA_STALE_ROOT'), a human-readable `message`, and a `recovery` hint. This directly addresses the #1 complaint pattern in ZKP SDKs: opaque 'proof verification failed' with no clue what went wrong.

title: Five-line hello-world with createSession() convenience API
description: Add a high-level createSession(humanSecret, agentCredential, opts?) function that handles nonce generation, both proofs, and verification in a single call returning a BolyraSession object. The current API requires 6+ calls across createHumanIdentity, createAgentCredential, proveHandshake (x2), and verifyHandshake. The convenience function reduces the quickstart from 25 lines to 5, dramatically lowering the 'time to first proof' for hackathon developers and LangChain/CrewAI integrators who just want auth to work.

title: Fluent delegation chain builder with compile-time scope validation
description: Add a DelegationBuilder class: `Delegation.from(parentCredential).narrowScope(['READ_DATA', 'WRITE_DATA']).expiresBefore(parentExpiry - 3600).to(delegateeCredential).build()`. The builder validates cumulative-bit invariants at build time (bit 4 implies 2+3), checks scope is a proper subset, and checks expiry narrowing — all before generating the circuit witness. This catches the three most common delegation mistakes (expanding scope, violating cumulative encoding, extending expiry) with clear TypeScript compiler errors instead of cryptic circuit constraint failures.

title: SD-JWT session token for off-chain proof reuse
description: After a successful handshake, emit an SD-JWT (Selective Disclosure JWT) session token that encodes the verified nullifiers, scope commitment, and expiry. Subsequent API calls within the session present the compact JWT instead of re-running the full ZKP verification. This bridges the gap between ZKP correctness and practical latency requirements: the initial handshake takes ~2s with rapidsnark, but follow-up calls verify in <1ms. The Python SDK already depends on PyJWT; add sd-jwt support to both SDKs with a verifySessionToken() helper.

title: Off-chain JWT session token from verified handshake
description: After a successful on-chain handshake verification, emit a signed JWT containing the nullifierHash, scopeCommitment, and sessionNonce. LangChain/CrewAI agents can then use this JWT as a bearer token for subsequent tool calls within the session window without re-proving on-chain. Add a `bolyra.session_token()` method to both TS and Python SDKs that wraps `verifyHandshake()` output into a compact JWT with configurable TTL (default 5 min). This eliminates the biggest adoption barrier: framework users don't want to call a blockchain for every tool invocation.

title: LangChain callback handler for automatic auth gating
description: Build a `BolyraCallbackHandler` that hooks into LangChain's `on_tool_start` event and checks the agent's Bolyra session token before allowing sensitive tool execution. This is more idiomatic than the existing `BolyraAuthTool` approach because it works transparently across all tools in a chain without requiring each tool to know about Bolyra. The handler reads required permission bits from a tool metadata decorator (`@bolyra_requires(permissions=0b00000100)`) and validates the scopeCommitment covers them. Ship as `bolyra-langchain` extras in the Python SDK.

title: CrewAI task decorator mapping crew roles to delegation scopes
description: CrewAI's task delegation model maps naturally to Bolyra's Delegation circuit: a manager agent delegates to a worker agent with narrowed permissions. Build a `@bolyra_delegated(max_scope=0b00000011)` task decorator that automatically generates a delegation proof when CrewAI's `Task.delegate()` is called, using the delegator's existing credential. The decorator should fail fast with a clear error if the requested scope exceeds the delegator's scope (before hitting the circuit). Include a worked example with a 3-agent crew: researcher (READ_DATA), analyst (READ+WRITE), and manager (all three).

title: Standardized proof envelope with MIME content-type for HTTP transport
description: Define an `application/bolyra-proof+json` content type and a JSON envelope schema (`{version, circuit, publicSignals, proof, sessionToken?}`) that framework HTTP tools can send/receive. Currently each integration reinvents serialization. A standard envelope lets any LangChain `RequestsTool`, CrewAI `HttpTool`, or AutoGen `HttpAgent` carry Bolyra proofs in a single header or body field. Add `BolyraProofEnvelope.serialize()` / `.deserialize()` to both SDKs and publish a JSON Schema file in `spec/`.

title: Five-line quickstart for framework users with pre-built test fixtures
description: Framework developers need to try Bolyra in under 60 seconds without running a local blockchain or compiling circuits. Ship a `bolyra.testing` module (both TS and Python) with pre-generated test identities, mock Merkle trees, and a `MockVerifier` that accepts any structurally valid proof. The quickstart should be literally 5 lines: `from bolyra.testing import alice_human, bob_agent; proof = bob_agent.prove_handshake(alice_human); assert proof.valid`. This mirrors how `langchain.testing` and `crewai` ship test fixtures. Gate the mock behind an explicit import path so it can't leak into production.

title: Cross-chain Merkle root sync via EIP-1186 storage proofs
description: Deploy IdentityRegistry on Arbitrum and Polygon alongside the existing Base Sepolia deployment, and implement a StorageProofRootRelay contract that accepts EIP-1186 storage proofs to sync humanTree and agentTree roots across chains without a trusted relayer. The relay verifies the RLP-encoded account/storage proof against the source chain's state root (available via L1 block hash oracles on each L2), then updates the local root history buffer. This eliminates bridge trust assumptions and lets a human enrolled on Base prove uniqueness on Arbitrum within one L1 finality window.

title: Bind sessionNonce to chain ID to prevent cross-chain replay
description: The current nullifierHash and sessionNonce have no chain binding — a valid handshake proof generated for Base can be replayed on Arbitrum if roots are synced. Fix by computing nonceBinding = Poseidon3(nullifierHash, sessionNonce, chainId) in HumanUniqueness and AgentPolicy circuits, adding chainId as a public input. The IdentityRegistry contract passes block.chainid into verification. This is a breaking circuit change but prevents a critical cross-chain replay vector that becomes exploitable the moment multi-chain deployment ships.

title: Gas benchmark suite for Groth16 verification across L2s
description: Build a Hardhat script that deploys the three verifier contracts (Human, Agent, Delegation) to forked Base, Arbitrum, and Polygon mainnets, runs verifyProof with test vectors, and reports gas costs per chain in a markdown table. L2s price the bn128 precompiles differently (Arbitrum charges ArbGas, Polygon uses different L1 data cost formulas), and the handshake requires 2-3 proof verifications per session. This benchmark identifies whether any chain makes full handshake verification prohibitively expensive and informs which chain to recommend as primary.

title: Multi-chain root registry with canonical chain designation
description: Create a RootRegistry contract deployed on each target L2 that maintains a mapping of (sourceChainId => treeType => root[30]) — a per-chain root history buffer. Enrollment happens on the canonical chain (Base), and roots propagate to satellite chains via storage proofs or an optimistic relay with fraud proofs. The IdentityRegistry on satellite chains accepts proofs against any root in the buffer for the canonical chain. This separates enrollment (write path, single chain) from verification (read path, any chain), enabling cross-chain handshakes without requiring users to enroll on every chain.

title: Portable delegation chains across L2s
description: Extend the Delegation circuit to accept an optional sourceChainId private input and output a chainAgnosticDelegationNullifier = Poseidon2(delegationNullifier, 0) alongside the chain-bound one. The on-chain contract on each L2 tracks both nullifier sets: the chain-bound one prevents local replay, the chain-agnostic one (synced via the root registry) prevents an agent from using the same delegation grant on multiple chains simultaneously. Without this, a delegated agent credential valid on Base could be independently exercised on Arbitrum and Polygon, tripling its effective scope.

title: Add domain separation tags to all nullifier hash computations
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier as Poseidon2(scope, secret), AgentPolicy as Poseidon2(secret, sessionNonce), and Delegation as Poseidon3(delegatorCredCommitment, delegateeCredCommitment, sessionNonce). These share the same Poseidon hash without domain separation, creating a theoretical collision risk across circuit types. Prepend a unique domain tag constant (e.g., 0x01 for human, 0x02 for agent, 0x03 for delegation) as the first Poseidon input in each nullifier computation. This is a one-line change per circuit plus updating the corresponding test vectors and verifier contracts.

title: Verify expiry comparison uses strict less-than with field-safe comparator
description: AgentPolicy checks currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp using circomlib's LessThan(64). However, the circuit must guarantee that both inputs are range-checked BEFORE the comparison — if the range check (Num2Bits) and the LessThan are not properly ordered by the constraint system, a malicious prover could supply a value >= 2^64 that wraps in the comparator. Write a formal property (as a Circomspect or Picus assertion) proving that for all satisfying witnesses, currentTimestamp < expiryTimestamp holds as a natural integer inequality, not just a field relation. Extend the same property to Delegation's delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry check.

title: Prove scope monotonicity across multi-hop delegation chains
description: The Delegation circuit checks delegateeScope & ~delegatorScope == 0 (subset) per hop, but there is no formal proof that this composes correctly across 3 hops to guarantee transitive monotonicity. Write a formal inductive argument (implemented as a Circomspect property or a standalone Python/Sage proof) showing that if hop_i.delegateeScope ⊆ hop_i.delegatorScope for each i, then hop_3.delegateeScope ⊆ hop_0.delegatorScope. Also verify that the cumulative bit encoding invariant (bit 4 → bits 2+3, bit 3 → bit 2) is preserved through each hop — a delegator with bits {4,3,2} cannot produce a delegatee with {3} alone (bit 2 must also be set).

title: Audit BinaryMerkleRoot for underconstrained depth input
description: All three circuits use BinaryMerkleRoot(MAX_DEPTH) with a variable merkleProofLength input. The @zk-kit implementation uses a Mux1 selector per level to either hash the sibling or pass through. If merkleProofLength is not range-checked to [1, MAX_DEPTH], a prover could set depth=0 making the 'root' equal to the leaf itself, bypassing Merkle inclusion entirely. Verify whether @zk-kit/binary-merkle-root.circom internally constrains depth, and if not, add explicit range checks: merkleProofLength >= 1 AND merkleProofLength <= MAX_DEPTH via LessThan/GreaterEqThan comparators. Write a negative test case that attempts depth=0 and confirms it fails.

title: Machine-checkable circuit invariant property file for all circuits
description: Create a circuits/FORMAL-PROPERTIES.md (or extend the existing stub) with machine-checkable invariant properties expressed in a format compatible with Circomspect or Picus. Properties to cover: (1) no signal can exceed BN254 field order r, (2) nullifierHash is deterministic given (identity, scope/nonce), (3) credentialCommitment uniquely binds all 5 credential fields, (4) Merkle root output changes if any sibling changes (non-malleability), (5) scope commitment binds both the bitmask and the credential identity. Provide a CI script that runs Circomspect --sarif on all .circom files and gates on zero high-severity findings.

title: Batch Poseidon hashes in AgentPolicy to reduce constraint count
description: AgentPolicy computes credentialCommitment (Poseidon5), nullifierHash (Poseidon2), and scopeCommitment (Poseidon2) as three separate Poseidon instances, each allocating its own round-constant wiring. Refactor to use a single Poseidon(5) for the credential and chain the two Poseidon(2) calls through shared intermediate signals, eliminating redundant range-check constraints on inputs that are already constrained. This should save ~600 constraints and measurably reduce witness generation time.

title: Eliminate redundant Num2Bits range checks across circuits
description: AgentPolicy applies Num2Bits(64) to permissionBitmask, expiryTimestamp, AND currentTimestamp. The currentTimestamp is a public input set by the verifier/relayer — range-checking it inside the circuit wastes 64 constraints since the contract can validate it cheaply in Solidity. Similarly, in Delegation.circom both delegatorScope and delegateeScope get Num2Bits(64) plus the bitmask bits are decomposed again for the subset check. Reuse the bit decomposition from the range check for the bitwise AND, saving ~128 constraints per circuit.

title: Replace bitwise scope subset with lookup-based check for PLONK builds
description: The cumulative bit encoding enforcement in Delegation.circom uses 3 separate IsZero + AND gate chains (bit4→bits2,3; bit3→bit2). For the PLONK build path, replace this with a custom gate or lookup table over the 256 valid 8-bit scope patterns. This reduces the cumulative encoding check from ~20 constraints to 1 lookup constraint in PLONK mode, and makes adding new implication rules zero-cost. The Groth16 path keeps the existing logic unchanged.

title: Reduce MAX_DEPTH for Delegation's delegatee Merkle proof
description: Delegation.circom uses MAX_DEPTH=20 for the delegatee Merkle inclusion proof (same as the agent tree), but the delegatee is always an agent in the agentTree. The BinaryMerkleRoot template allocates MAX_DEPTH Poseidon(2) hashes regardless of actual tree depth. If the agent tree is expected to stay under 2^16 entries for the foreseeable future, instantiating Delegation with MAX_DEPTH=16 saves 4 × ~300 = 1,200 constraints and proportionally reduces proving time. Add a compile-time constant AGENT_TREE_DEPTH separate from HUMAN_TREE_DEPTH.

title: Share EdDSA verification infrastructure in ModelInstanceBinding
description: ModelInstanceBinding runs two full EdDSAPoseidonVerifier instances (~6,000 constraints each) for operator and provider signatures, both over the same credentialCommitment message. The Poseidon hash of the message (used internally by EdDSA verify) is computed twice. Extract the message hash as a shared signal and feed it into both verifiers, saving ~300 constraints. Additionally, the two BabyCheck point-on-curve checks for the two public keys share identical constraint structure — verify this doesn't cause underconstrained aliasing when both keys happen to be equal (operator == provider edge case).

title: Off-chain session token with on-chain checkpoint batching
description: After a successful handshake, emit a short-lived JWT-like session token (signed by the verifier) that agents can present for subsequent calls without re-proving. Batch session roots on-chain every N minutes via a Merkle accumulator. This drops per-call cost from ~300k gas to near-zero for repeat interactions, which is table-stakes for agentic commerce where agents make dozens of tool calls per task. The IdentityRegistry already tracks sessionNonce; extend it with a sessionTokenRoot public mapping and a batchCheckpoint() function.

title: Bind handshake proof to payment channel authorization
description: Extend the AgentPolicy circuit's scopeCommitment to include a paymentChannelId (e.g., a state channel or X402 payment hash). This lets a single proof simultaneously authorize identity AND a payment ceiling — the killer feature for agentic commerce where every API call costs money. Add a Poseidon4(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, paymentChannelId, maxAmount) output signal. The 8-bit permission model already has FINANCIAL_SMALL/MEDIUM/UNLIMITED tiers; this makes them enforceable at the cryptographic layer rather than relying on application-level checks.

title: Swarm handshake: one human proof authorizes N agents atomically
description: In real agentic workflows (CrewAI, AutoGen), a human dispatches 3-8 agents simultaneously. Today each agent needs a separate handshake, which is O(N) proving time and gas. Add a SwarmHandshake mode where the human proves once and the contract accepts a batch of agent proofs bound to the same sessionNonce. The IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake already validates nonce freshness; extend it with a verifySwarmHandshake(humanProof, agentProofs[]) that checks all agent proofs share the same nonce and marks them atomically. This is the difference between 'cool demo' and 'production-ready for LangGraph'.

title: Real-time credential revocation event feed for agent operators
description: Agent operators (enterprises running fleets) need to revoke a compromised agent key and have all in-flight sessions reject it within seconds, not whenever the next Merkle root propagates. Add a revocation bitmap indexed by credentialCommitment that the IdentityRegistry checks during verifyHandshake and verifyDelegation. Emit RevocationBitmapUpdated events so off-chain verifiers can subscribe. The agentTree root history buffer (30 roots) means a revoked agent could still prove against a stale root for up to 30 enrollments — the bitmap closes this window to one block.

title: Human tree root history buffer (parity with agent tree)
description: The agentTree has a 30-root history buffer but the humanTree has none — any new human enrollment instantly invalidates all in-flight human proofs. For a protocol targeting mainstream adoption where enrollments happen continuously, this is a showstopper. Add the same ROOT_HISTORY_SIZE=30 ring buffer pattern from agentTree to humanTree in IdentityRegistry.sol. Update enrollHuman() to push to the buffer and add an isKnownHumanRoot() check in verifyHandshake(). This is a critical correctness gap that will surface the moment enrollment rate exceeds one per proof-generation window (~15 seconds).

title: Nullifier lacks cross-circuit domain separation
description: HumanUniqueness computes nullifier = Poseidon2(scope, secret) and AgentPolicy computes nullifier = Poseidon2(sessionNonce, credentialCommitment). If scope and sessionNonce collide (both are public inputs chosen externally), and if secret happens to equal a credentialCommitment, nullifiers collide across circuit types. More critically, there is no domain tag distinguishing human vs agent vs delegation nullifiers — the contract uses separate mappings (usedNonces vs usedDelegationNullifiers) but a collision in the field could cause cross-protocol interference. Fix: prepend a circuit-specific domain separator constant as the first Poseidon input, e.g. Poseidon3(HUMAN_DOMAIN, scope, secret).

title: Agent expiry check is prover-controlled — no on-chain freshness enforcement
description: AgentPolicy takes currentTimestamp as a public input and checks expiryTimestamp > currentTimestamp inside the circuit. However, the prover chooses currentTimestamp. The contract has an AgentTimestampStale error defined but it's unclear if the contract actually validates that the proof's currentTimestamp is within a tight window of block.timestamp. If missing, a prover can set currentTimestamp = 0 and use an expired credential indefinitely. Audit and enforce that IdentityRegistry.verifyHandshake checks |pubSignals.currentTimestamp - block.timestamp| < MAX_CLOCK_DRIFT (e.g., 300 seconds) for both agent and delegation proofs.

title: Human tree has no root history buffer — proofs go stale on any enrollment
description: The contract maintains a 30-root history buffer for agentTree but humanTree has none — the contract checks against humanTree.root directly. Any new human enrollment changes the root, instantly invalidating all in-flight human proofs. In a system with concurrent enrollments, this creates a denial-of-service vector: an attacker who can enroll humans can repeatedly invalidate honest users' proofs. Add a matching 30-root ring buffer for humanTree roots, identical to the existing agentRootHistory pattern.

title: Scope commitment is brute-forceable (8-bit permission space)
description: AgentPolicy outputs scopeCommitment = Poseidon2(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment). The permissionBitmask is only 8 meaningful bits (256 values). Given a scopeCommitment and credentialCommitment (which is the Merkle leaf, potentially extractable from on-chain events), an observer can brute-force all 256 possible bitmasks to recover the agent's exact permissions, breaking the privacy model. Fix: add a random blinding salt as a third input — scopeCommitment = Poseidon3(permissionBitmask, credentialCommitment, blindingSalt) — making brute-force infeasible.

title: Verify delegation expiry narrowing is constrained (delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry)
description: The Delegation circuit takes both delegatorExpiry and delegateeExpiry as private inputs and should enforce delegateeExpiry <= delegatorExpiry (a delegatee cannot extend their credential lifetime beyond the delegator's). The circuit code was truncated in the prompt, so this constraint may or may not exist. If missing, a delegatee can self-grant a longer-lived credential than the delegator authorized. Audit the Delegation circuit for a LessThan comparison on expiry fields and add one if absent. This is analogous to the scope-narrowing subset check but for the temporal dimension.
